preFix.title: Throwing a stoneware butter dish with lid
preFix.subtitle: A wide shallow base and a domed lid with gallery fit, fired to cone 6 for everyday use
preFix.excerpt: A two-part stoneware butter dish: a shallow open base approximately 130 mm wide and 20 mm deep, and a domed lid with a flat gallery that sits inside the base rim with 2 mm clearance. Calipers are used throughout to achieve a close-fitting lid. Glaze-fired to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C), food-safe and dishwasher safe.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln.

A stoneware butter dish is a practical object where lid fit matters. The base is thrown as a wide shallow bowl just deep enough for a 250 g block of butter to sit flat. The domed lid has a flat gallery on its underside that sits inside the base rim with 2 mm clearance. Both pieces are measured with calipers at the throwing stage to ensure that clearance is maintained through firing shrinkage.
Glaze-fired
to
cone
6 (approximately 1220 degrees C), the finished dish is food-safe and dishwasher safe.

materials.name: Stoneware clay, smooth
materials.quantity: 800 g (base 350 g, lid 300 g, trimmings 150 g)

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: as needed for dipping or brushing

content.text: Preparation

A butter dish lid must fit inside the base rather than sitting on top of it: the gallery on the lid underside registers against the base rim interior. This means the lid must be thrown wider than the base rim opening, and then trimmed back with a gallery. Throw base and lid in the same session so both dry at the same rate and can be checked against each other at
leather-hard
. Wedge all clay thoroughly before centering.
Throwing the body
Centre 350 g of stoneware on a bat. Open the base wide: press outward from the centre to create a floor approximately 110 mm in diameter, leaving approximately 8 mm floor thickness.
Use
pulling a wall

to raise the rim: you need only low walls, approximately 20 mm tall, around the wide base. The rim should be approximately 130 mm in diameter when finished. Keep the walls at 6 mm thickness: the dish is shallow and wide, so walls need to be robust.
Compress the interior floor firmly with a flat sponge to consolidate the clay and reduce cracking during drying. Smooth the inner walls with the wooden rib.
Measure the interior diameter of the rim with calipers and note the measurement. This is the reference dimension for the lid gallery.
Wire off the base dish from the bat and leave on the bat to dry to leather-hard. Do not move it while still plastic: the wide shallow form distorts easily.
Throwing the lid

Centre 300 g of clay. Open and throw a wide low dome: the outer diameter should be approximately 140 mm (larger than the base rim so the gallery can be trimmed to fit inside). The dome height should be approximately 40 mm at the centre. Walls 5 mm thick.

Throw a knob on the centre of the dome: pull up a solid cylinder approximately 20 mm tall from the centre, narrow it to approximately 14 mm diameter, and round or flatten the top. Smooth all surfaces with a damp chamois.
Wire off and leave on the bat alongside the base to reach the same leather-hard stage.
Trimming and handles
Trim the base at leather-hard: invert on the banding wheel and trim a
foot-ring

approximately 8 mm wide and 5 mm tall. The foot-ring diameter should sit approximately 15 mm inside the outer rim, leaving the base floor slightly domed rather than flat to prevent any wobble on an uneven surface.

Trim the lid gallery: invert the leather-hard lid dome on the banding wheel, resting the knob on a soft pad. Using the loop tool, trim a flat gallery approximately 8 mm wide on the underside edge of the lid. The gallery should project downward approximately 10 mm from the lid edge. The outer edge of the gallery, when placed inside the base, should be 2 mm smaller in radius than the base interior rim measurement taken with the calipers.

Check the fit: place the lid gallery-side down into the base opening. It should drop in smoothly with approximately 2 mm clearance all round and rest on the gallery without tilting. If the gallery is too tight, trim more. If too loose, the lid will be sloppy in use: clay cannot be added back at this stage, so continue to use that lid for a different base.
Drying and bisque firing
Dry both pieces together covered loosely with plastic for 2 days, then uncover and allow to reach
bone-dry
at room temperature. Load the base upright on the kiln shelf. Load the lid resting in the base in the gallery position.
Bisque
fire to approximately 1000 degrees C. Firing lid in base ensures both shrink together and the gallery fit is preserved.
Glazing
Wax the foot-ring of the base and the underside of the lid gallery with wax resist. These surfaces must remain unglazed to prevent sticking to the kiln shelf or to the base during the
glaze firing

Glaze the interior of the base by dipping or applying with a mop brush in two to three overlapping coats. Glaze the exterior walls. Check the foot-ring is clear of glaze; wipe off any overspray with a damp sponge.

Glaze the exterior of the lid and the knob. Confirm the gallery underside is unglazed. Apply alumina wash to the base interior rim surface where the gallery will rest during firing, as an additional insurance against sticking.
Glaze firing
Fire the base and lid separately in the
glaze firing
(unlike the bisque firing, do not nest them together with glaze applied). Place the lid knob-side down on a kiln stilt if needed, or upright on the shelf. Fire to
cone
6 (approximately 1220 degrees C). Allow to cool to below 100 degrees C before opening the kiln.
Care

Glazed stoneware fired to cone 6 is food-safe, dishwasher safe, and microwave safe. The butter dish base can go in the dishwasher with the lid placed separately. The unglazed foot-ring may mark slightly on polished surfaces; a felt pad on the base foot prevents scratching.
Variations
Cheese bell: scale up the lid dome to approximately 150 mm wide and 80 mm tall with the same gallery system, sized to sit over a cheese board section.
Oval base: throw a round base then gently elongate it on the bat to an oval before the first drying; use a matching oval lid template.
Two-glaze finish: glaze the exterior in a darker colour and the interior in a lighter one. This makes it easy to see the food contact surface at a glance.
Salt dish: use the same base form at 100 mm wide and 15 mm deep without a lid as an open salt cellar or condiment dish.

rows.fix: Compress the base floor firmly before leaving to dry. Cover loosely with plastic and dry slowly over 3 to 4 days. Dry both pieces rim-down on absorbent board to equalise moisture loss
rows.cause: Wide shallow forms dry unevenly; the rim dries before the floor, setting up tension
rows.problem: Base dish warping or cracking during drying

rows.fix: When trimming the gallery, set it 2 to 3 mm looser than the caliper measurement to account for the possibility of differential shrinkage. If already tight after firing, light wet sanding of the gallery edge with 220 grit paper sometimes eases the fit
rows.cause: Gallery trimmed to exact caliper measurement without allowing for shrinkage differential
rows.problem: Lid gallery too tight to fit into base after bisque firing

rows.fix: Trim the gallery on the banding wheel at a slow, consistent speed and use the loop tool in a single direction. Check the gallery sits flat by resting the inverted lid on a flat tile and checking for wobble before curing
rows.cause: Gallery underside not flat and level after trimming, or base interior rim not level from throwing
rows.problem: Lid rocking inside the base rather than sitting level

rows.fix: Wipe bisqueware with a damp sponge before glazing to remove dust. Apply glaze immediately and do not re-handle the glazed interior surface. If crawling has already occurred, the piece can be re-glazed and re-fired once with the bare spot re-coated
rows.cause: Dust or finger grease on the bisque surface in that area preventing glaze adhesion
rows.problem: Glaze crawling off the interior floor of the base in the glaze firing
